title: Quiz: Classical Boltzmann Machines
description: Test your understanding of classical Boltzmann machines and RBMs

questions.question: What determines the probability of a configuration in a Boltzmann machine?

options.text: The energy function through the Boltzmann distribution

questions.explanation: The probability of a configuration is proportional to exp(-E/T), where E is the energy function and T is temperature. Lower energy configurations have higher probability.

questions.question: What restriction makes RBMs computationally tractable compared to full Boltzmann machines?

options.text: No connections within visible or hidden layers

questions.explanation: RBMs remove intra-layer connections (visible-visible and hidden-hidden), making hidden units conditionally independent given visible units. This enables parallel Gibbs sampling.

questions.question: What does Contrastive Divergence approximate?

options.text: The number of hidden units needed

questions.explanation: CD approximates the gradient of the log-likelihood by using short Gibbs chains starting from data, avoiding the need to sample from the equilibrium distribution.

questions.question: In the CD-k gradient, what do the positive and negative phases compute?
questions.explanation: The positive phase computes expectations under the data distribution (⟨·⟩_data), while the negative phase approximates expectations under the model distribution (⟨·⟩_model) using k Gibbs steps.

questions.question: In an RBM, hidden units are conditionally independent given the visible units.
questions.explanation: True. The bipartite structure of RBMs (no hidden-hidden connections) means P(h|v) factors as a product: P(h|v) = ∏_j P(h_j|v). This is the key property that makes RBMs tractable.

questions.question: What is the free energy in an RBM?

options.text: The energy averaged over hidden units
The negative log of the marginalized probability over hidden units

questions.explanation: Free energy F(v) = -log Σ_h exp(-E(v,h)) is the negative log of the unnormalized probability P(v). It can be computed analytically for RBMs.

questions.question: Why is parallel tempering used in Boltzmann machine sampling?

options.text: To escape local minima and improve mixing
To reduce the number of hidden units

questions.explanation: Parallel tempering runs chains at different temperatures. High-temperature chains can escape local minima more easily, and configuration swaps help the low-temperature chains explore better.

questions.question: Which are limitations of classical Boltzmann machines that quantum versions aim to address? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Getting stuck in local energy minima

questions.explanation: Classical BMs suffer from (a) slow mixing due to local updates, (b) intractable partition function for large systems, and (d) difficulty escaping local minima. Quantum approaches address these through superposition and tunneling.
